coredata.dc:title: Superhydrophobic electrodeposited nano-sio2/N-doped carbon dots coating for Mg alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Xutong
Wang, Yongfei
Guo, Ruijie
Niu, Baolong
Wang, Huifang
Yan, Hong
coredata.dc:description: To solve the problems of poor corrosion resistance, low adhesion and poor abrasion resistance of general superhydrophobic coatings, A superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared on the surface of AZ31B magnesium alloy. The electrolyte consisted of nano-silica particles and N-CDs dispersion, where nano-SiO2 particles were innovatively added to improve the abrasion resistance of the coating. After the superhydrophobic modification by 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PTES), the coating exhibited a water contact angle (WCA) of 154° and a rolling angle (RA) of 7°. The analysis undertaken via potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) demonstrated improved electrochemical performance for the coated magnesium alloy samples when compared with uncoated counterparts. The assemblies formed by the coordination of Mg2+ with the functional groups of N-CD (−NH2, –COOH and –OH) were electrodeposited onto the surface of the magnesium alloy matrix, achieving a dense protective film and thereby slowing down the corrosion rate. At the same time, the abrasion resistance test results indicated that the prepared coating possessed excellent abrasion resistance, possessing the ability to resist damage to the coating caused by friction to a certain extent, proposing a new idea for fabricating superhydrophobic coatings with increased abrasion resistance on Mg alloys.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium alloy
Superhydrophobic
N-doped carbon dots
Electrodeposition
Abrasion resistance

If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.The work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22178242) and the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (202102040201009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Particle size distribution of synthesized N-CDs powders (a); XRD pattern of N-CDs coated magnesium alloy (b); XPS spectra of N-CDs coating obtained at 10 V for 10 min: Full survey (c); C 1s (d); O 1s (e); N 1s (f).
SEM images of nano SiO2/N-CDs coating surface electrodeposited for 10 min (a–c), 30 min (d–f) and PTES-modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min (h–i).
SEM images (a, g) and EDS mapping images (b–f, h–l) of the surface of the nano SiO2/N-CDs coating electrodeposited for 10 min.
SEM images (a, e), EDS mapping images (b, c, f, g), and EDS patterns of the surface of PTES modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min at different magnifications.
SEM images of the thickness of the nano SiO2/N-CDs coating after electrodeposition for 10 (a) and 30 min (b).

EDS surface scan of a cross-section of the nano SiO2/N-CDs coating electrodeposited for 30 min: (a, b, g, h, m, n) Surface scan regions; elemental distribution: (c, i, o) C; (d, j, p) O; (e, k, q) N; (f, l, r) Si.
XPS spectra of SiO2/N-CDs coating obtained at10 V for 10 min: (a) Full survey; (b) C 1s; (c) O 1s; (d) N 1s; (e) Si 2p.

Water contact angles of different samples: Bare magnesium alloy (a); Nano SiO2/N-CDs coating electrodeposited for 10 min (b); PTES-modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min (c); Rolling angle of superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating electrodeposited for 10 min (d–f).
Self-cleaning test procedure with superhydrophobic SiO2/N-CDs coatings electrodeposited for 10 min on flat surfaces (a–e) and inclined surfaces (f–j).
EIS test results for different samples: (a) Nyquist plots; (b) Bode plots of |Z| versus frequency; (c) Bode plots of phase angle versus frequency.

EIS equivalent circuit model fitting results for different coated samples; (a) Nyquist plots; (b) Bode plots of |Z| versus frequency; (c) Bode plots of phase angle versus frequency; (d) Equivalent circuit model for fitting the corresponding EIS curve.
Dynamic potential polarization curves of different samples in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

Adhesion resistance test of PTES-modified nano SiO2/N-CDs coating electrodeposited for 10 min: X-cut tape test (a); Cross-cut tape test (b); Abrasion resistance test procedure of the coatings: Test start (c); Test in progress (d).
Changes in WCA in wear resistance tests of superhydrophobic SiO2/N-CDs coatings electrodeposited for 10 min.
Equivalent component fitting parameters for EIS equivalent circuit models for different coated samples.
Corrosion parameters calculated by Tafel extrapolation method for different samples.

row.entry: PTES modified superhydrophobic coating- electrodeposition 10 min

head.ce:title: Superhydrophobic electrodeposited nano-sio2/N-doped carbon dots coating for Mg alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials (Taiyuan University of Technology)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, 030024, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To solve the problems of poor corrosion resistance, low adhesion and poor abrasion resistance of general superhydrophobic coatings, A superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was prepared on the surface of AZ31B magnesium alloy. The electrolyte consisted of nano-silica particles and N-CDs dispersion, where nano-SiO2 particles were innovatively added to improve the abrasion resistance of the coating. After the superhydrophobic modification by 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PTES), the coating exhibited a water contact angle (WCA) of 154° and a rolling angle (RA) of 7°. The analysis undertaken via potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) demonstrated improved electrochemical performance for the coated magnesium alloy samples when compared with uncoated counterparts. The assemblies formed by the coordination of Mg2+ with the functional groups of N-CD (−NH2, –COOH and –OH) were electrodeposited onto the surface of the magnesium alloy matrix, achieving a dense protective film and thereby slowing down the corrosion rate. At the same time, the abrasion resistance test results indicated that the prepared coating possessed excellent abrasion resistance, possessing the ability to resist damage to the coating caused by friction to a certain extent, proposing a new idea for fabricating superhydrophobic coatings with increased abrasion resistance on Mg alloys.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The magnesium alloy stands acknowledged as the lightest metallic substance prevalent currently. It finds extensive application in sectors encompassing automobiles, aerospace, marine transport, defense industry, electronics, biomedicine, etc., underpinned by its low density, elevated specific strength, and facile recyclability. However, it grapples with significant oxidation and corrosion tendencies owing to its reactive chemical attributes, restricting its broad utilization [1–4]. Consequently, augmenting the corrosion resistance of magnesium alloys becomes paramount. As reported, there are several surface treatments to improve the corrosion resistance of magnesium alloys such as anti-corrosive coating [1], self-healing coating [1,5,6], gasochromic switchable films [1,7] and superhydrophobic coating [1,5–8]. Specifically, a superhydrophobic coating can exhibit several beneficial properties on metallic surfaces, including self-cleaning, reduced fluidic drag, and enhanced corrosion resistance. These advantages are achieved through sophisticated surface engineering techniques that create the desired superhydrophobic characteristics [1].

As two new types of green corrosion inhibitors, carbon dots (CDs) and imidazole have drawn extensive attention. Specifically, leveraging on the benefits of abundant raw materials, superior water solubility, eco-friendliness, affordability, and simplicity in synthesis, CDs have attained prominence for their application in corrosion resistance studies [9]. CDs belong to the carbonaceous family of materials, which exhibit remarkable properties and advantages such as excellent optical and chemical stability, short response time, high water solubility, flexible preparation and functionalization, environmentally friendly synthesis and reduction in toxicity [10–12]. Zhao et al. prepared nitrogen and sulfur codoped carbon dots with good water dispersibility by a hydrothermal method [13]. Being a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with a size under 10 nm, CDs contain abundant –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 functional groups disseminated across their surfaces, which make them possess high water-solubility, low biotoxicity, and high light stability and thus have shown enormous potential in extensive areas [14–18]. Additionally, nitrogen is an excellent doping element and emerging applications of N-doped carbon dots are being explored in corrosion protection, leading to new functions and properties [16,17]. The prevalence of isolated electron pairs within nitrogen-bearing functional groups imparts potential active sites [11]. Consequently, N-CDs are endowed with both the superior qualities of carbondots and the ability to mitigate metal corrosion rates within corrosive environments [9,19,20]. Cui et al. successfully fabricated novel N-CDs via a hydrothermal strategy, demonstrating that these synthesized N-CDs possess the capacity to adsorb onto steel surfaces, thereby effectively isolating the corrosive environment [21]. Padhan et al. explore the efficacy of N-CDs and Cu, N-doped carbon dots (Cu, N-CDs), featuring diverse levels of copper doping, as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel substrates [12]. However, limited work exists regarding the N-CD's role in Mg alloy corrosion resistance.

Recent studies indicate that the inclusion of nanoparticle dopants can scatter dislocations and strengthen the electroplated layer, making it more durable against abrasion and corrosion [22,23]. Qin et al. synthesized a SiO2–Ni dual-composite coating onto the AZ91D Mg alloy via the advanced electrodeposition technique. They systematically studied the microstructure, mechanical, tribological and corrosion performances of as-prepared coating with different SiO2 doping concentrations and found SiO2 doping can improve the wear resistance and corrosion resistance of Ni-based coatings [22]. Typically, ceramic fillers such as TiO2, SiC, Si3N4, and Al2O3 are selected for this purpose [22,24,25]. Of these fillers, SiO2 is a highly cost-effective option that possesses remarkable wear resistance and chemical stability, lending itself readily to modification and customization [23,26,27].

Herein, the superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating was designed and fabricated on the Mg alloy substrate to improve the corrosion and abrasion resistance, which includes the electrodeposition of nano-SiO2/N-CDs and the superhydrophobic modification by 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PTES). The corrosion resistance property was achieved by the electrodeposited nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating, where nano-SiO2 offer enhanced abrasion resistance property. The superhydrophobicity was obtained by the modification with low-energy materials of PTES. The as-prepared PTES modified nano-SiO2/N-CDs coatings demonstrated good electrochemical performance, self-cleaning performance and remarkable abrasion resistance. This work not only provides a new approaching for fabricating anticorrosive coatings on Mg alloys but also sheds new light on the improvement in abrasion resistance of superhydrophobic coating.

AZ31B magnesium alloys with a size of 20 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm were bought from Dongguan Kuangyu Metal Material Co., Ltd., China. Anhydrous alcohol was from Tianjin Beichen District Fangzheng Reagent Factory. Acetone was from Sinopharm Group Shanxi Co., Ltd., China. Citric acid was from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Benzotriazole and nano-SiO2 powder were from Aladdin. Methanol was from Maclean. Sodium dodecyl sulfonate was from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PTES) was from Bide Pharmatech Ltd. The chemicals utilized herein were all of analytical grade, and solution preparation was executed exclusively using ultrapure deionized water.

Magnesium alloys (20 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm) were polished using SiC sandpaper (grade 400, 800, 1200, and 2000) to eliminate the existing oxide film and surface rusting. Post-polished, these magnesium alloy specimens were subjected to ultrasonic cleaning utilizing acetone and anhydrous ethanol, respectively, for 5 min at room temperature and then dried naturally.

The preparation of N-CDs was achieved via a hydrothermal technique. A quantity of 7.5 g of citric acid and an equivalent of 1.5 g of benzotriazole were solubilized in a volume of 200 mL of deionized water and then placed in a 250 mL PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor, and then hydrothermally reacted for 8 h at 180 °C. The obtained N-CDs dispersion was filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous membrane suction and collect the filtrate to remove any large impurities generated during the reaction.

The electrolyte was prepared by 100 mL N-CDs dispersion with 2 g of nano-silica and 1g of Sodium dodecyl sulfonate added, then stir them to well-mixed to create a well-mixed electrolyte. In reference to the electrodeposition segment, it involved utilizing a constant potential polarization method at an applied voltage level of 10 V. Here, the magnesium alloy served as the active electrode, a platinum plate of dimensions (20 mm × 20 mm × 0.1 mm) functioned as the opposing electrode, maintaining a separation of 2.5 cm. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was employed as the reference electrode. The electrodeposition time was set at 10 and 30 min. Finally, the magnesium alloy sample obtained was thoroughly washed using deionized water and then dried at room temperature.

At room temperature, the electrodeposited nano-SiO2/N-CDs coated samples were immersed in a 1 wt% 1H,1H,2H,2H-perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (PTES) solution in methanol, subsequently, triple molar excess of deionized water was added, and the hydrolysis was reacted for 1 h. After that, the samples were taken out and put into a blower drying oven at 140 °C for 1 h, thereby achieving superhydrophobicity.

Metal alloy samples' electrochemical properties were assessed via an electrochemical work station (CorrTest CS Studio 5.3). Specifically, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization curves evaluations were performed at a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under room conditions. A conventional three-electrode system was assembled utilizing a platinum sheet electrode (20 mm × 20 mm × 0.1 mm) as the counter electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and the sample under scrutiny as the working electrode. Prior to the EIS evaluation, the sample was soaked in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 1 h to attain a steady open-circuit voltage. Post completion of the EIS assessment, the scanning rate was adjusted to 5 mV/s, the initial potential was established at −2.5 V, and the termination potential was set to 0.1 V for the potentiodynamic polarization curve examination. Each experiment was repeated three times and the results were averaged.

Supernatant of synthetic N-CDs dispersion was used to analyze the particle size (Nanotracwave II, Microtrac Co., Ltd., USA). X-ray Diffraction (XRD) (DX-2800, Dandong Haoyuan Instruments Co., Ltd., China) at a scanning speed of 4°/min and a scanning range of 10–90° was used to analyze the composition of the N-CDs coating. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ThermoScientificK-Alpha) was used to analyze the chemical composition and element content of the N-CDs coating and the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating. The surface morphology and elemental distribution of the coating was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS). Each experiment was repeated three times and the results were averaged.

An optical contact angle measuring apparatus (model JC2000C1, manufactured by Shanghai Centron Digital Technology Co., Ltd., China) was employed for the precise determination of the water contact angle utilizing the goniometric methodology. This involved the utilization of 3 μL droplets of deionized water under standard room temperature and each water contact angle was measured at five different positions.

A minor quantity of N-CDs suspension was subjected to particle dimension analysis following filtration and ultrasound for a duration of 10 min. As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), the nanoscale average diameter of the produced N-CDs registered at 1.35 nm. The particle size profile exhibited normality, signifying that the resulting N-CDs possess a consistent size distribution.

To individually characterize the elemental composition of N-CDs of the coatings, N-CDs coatings were prepared on magnesium alloy substrates for XRD testing. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the XRD pattern of the coating showed that the weak XRD peak at about 43° is designated as the C(100) plane [28,29]. Due to the deep XRD signal, the peak of the magnesium alloy substrate affected the peak of the N-CDs coating. Currently, only the peak at 43° could be used to analyze N-CDs, and the remaining peaks are all the peaks of the magnesium alloy substrate.

To further characterize the chemical structure of N-CDs in coatings, XPS tests were carried out on N-CDs coated powders on magnesium alloy substrates. Fig. 1(c) was the result of the full spectrum of the coating. After calculation and analysis by Avantage software, the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, and N1s in the full spectrum were 52.6 %, 31.11 %, and 2.69 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1(d), CC/C–C (284.5 eV), C–O/C–N (286.1eV) and –COOH (288.5eV) could be observed in the C1s fine spectrum [10]. As shown in Fig. 1(e), the O1s fine spectrum was split into C–OH (533.2 eV) and C–O (531.4 eV) [10,30]. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the N1s fine spectrum showed peaks of N–H (399.5 eV), which were all derived from N-CDs [31].

Fig. 2 shows the surface SEM images of the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coatings electrodeposited for 10 and 30 min and PTES-modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min at different magnification, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(a–f), the surface microscopic morphology of the prepared nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating was uniform and dense. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the morphology of the coating electrodeposited for 10 min was spherical particles piled up at a scale of 1 μm, while the coating obtained after electrodeposition for 30 min is uneven, as shown in Fig. 2(f).

Fig. 2(h and i) show the surface SEM images of the PTES modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min magnification respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(i), the superhydrophobic coating modified by PTES has a uniform micro-nano structure.

Fig. 3 shows the EDS mapping of the electrodeposited 10 min coating at a scale of 2.5 and 500 μm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(c–e) and (i, k), the C and N from the N-CDs are uniformly distributed on the coating surface at a scale of 2.5 and 500 μm, indicating that the N-CDs are uniformly electrodeposited on the surface of the magnesium alloy. As shown in Fig. 3(f–l), the Si element from the nano-SiO2 is also uniformly distributed at a scale of 2.5 and 500 μm, indicating that the uniform doping of nano-silica is realized under the action of sodium dodecyl sulfonate, and the uniform nano-electrodeposition of the two substances is realized. The common O of the two substances of nano-SiO2 and N-CDs are also uniformly distributed at a scale of 2.5 and 500 μm, as shown in Fig. 3(j), which further proves that both N-CDs and nano-SiO2 are uniformly deposited on the surface of the magnesium alloy substrate.

Fig. 4 shows the EDS mapping of the surface of PTES modified coating electrodeposited for 10 min at 500 nm and 500 μm scales, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(c–g), the F element from PTES is uniformly distributed on the coating surface at 500 nm and 500 μm scales, indicating that PTES evenly covers the surface of the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the average thickness of the coating with electrodeposition time of 10 and 30 min reached about 218 and 469 μm, respectively. The coating was evenly and tightly packed on the cross section.

The results that whether the two substances of the coating achieved uniform distribution in the cross-section direction were shown in Fig. 6 . In the three small selection areas (the left, middle and right three small areas) of the cross-section, the four elements C, O, N, Si were uniformly deposited in the coating for 30 min.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the full spectrum of the coating. After calculation and analysis by Avantage software, the atomic percentages of C1s, O1s, N1s, and Si2p were 65.72 %, 24.76 %, 1.26 %, and 6.87 %.The peak splitting of the fine spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s in Fig. 7(b–d) was exactly the same as that in Fig. 7(b–d). They all came from N-CDs, which illustrates the realization of N-CDs deposition in the coating. As shown in Fig. 7(e), the fine spectra of Si2p show the peak of SiO2 at 102.9eV, proving that the coating realizes the doping of nano-SiO2 in N-CDs [32].

During the wettability test, the droplets on the electrodeposited 10 min nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating began to show hydrophobicity, and the water contact angle (WCA) became smaller in about 30 s, finally the WCA was stable at 115 ± 3.1°, as shown in Fig. 8 (b). Fig. 8(c) shows that the WCA of the electrodeposited 10 min superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating modified by PTES reached 154 ± 2.8°, exhibiting good superhydrophobicity.
Fig. 8(d–f) shows the rolling angle (RA) of water droplets on the inclined surface at 7°, indicating that the 10 min superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating has good superhydrophobic properties.

The superhydrophobic properties of the coating can also offer good self-cleaning performance. As shown in Fig. 9 (a–e), the PTES-modified coating electrodeposited 10 min was placed on a horizontal plane, and an appropriate amount of blue chalk ash was sprinkled on the surface of the coating. As the water drops dropped, the chalk ash was easily washed away, exhibiting that the coating showed good self-cleaning performance.

Then the electrodeposited 10 min superhydrophobic nano SiO2/N-CDs coating was placed on the inclined surface, as shown in Fig. 9(f–j). With the dripping of small water droplets, the blue chalk ash on the coating surface was cleaned with the rolling of the water droplets. The electrodeposited 10 min superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating exhibited excellent self-cleaning performance.

Dynamic potential polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) assessments were executed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution via an electrochemical workstation under ambient conditions. The electrode system was fabricated with a traditional three-electrode cell (platinum plate as the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and magnesium alloy sample as the working electrode). Prior to the test, immerse the sample into 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 20 min to attain a stable open-circuit voltage.

Fig. 10 (a) displays the Nyquist plots for the four groups of samples. The comparable trends demonstrates that they share similar semicircle shape, the same corrosion mechanism, while there are some distinctions in corrosion rates. The Nyquist plot's high-frequency semicircle shows the charge transfer resistance at the solid-liquid interface, and its diameter of the semicircle impedance arc denotes the polarization resistance. The PTES modified magnesium alloy sample exhibits elevated charge transfer and polarization resistances, suggesting improved electrochemical performance [33–36].

Fig. 10(b) exhibites the Bode plots of phase vs. frequency and the fitting circuits for the four sets of magnesium alloy samples. The impedance performance of PTES modified magnesium sample is improved as it possesses the highest phase in the high-frequency area [33,34,36].

Fig. 10(c) displays the Bode plots of impedance modulus and frequency for four groups of magnesium alloy samples. In general, |Z| = 0.01 Hz is used as a semi-quantitative indicator of the theoretical corrosion resistance of coatings as it is the impedance modulus in the lowest frequency area. The PTES modified superhydrophobic magnesium alloy sample exhibits the highest impedance modulus |Z| in the low-frequency region, which exhibits its enhanced electrochemical performance [33,34,36].

EIS diagrams of four sets of magnesium samples are fitted with a model in Fig. 11 (d). In the equivalent circuit, R c stands the coating's resistance. R s stands the impedance between the working electrode and the counter electrode in the electrolyte, C dl stands the double-layer capacitance between the coating of working electrode and the electrolyte, and R ct stands the charge transfer resistance between the coating and the substrate, which are all controlled by the dynamics of the electrochemical reaction. R L and LL are used to represent the resistance and inductive elements of the low-frequency induction loop. CPE dl is the constant phase unit of the analog coating [37,38].

As shown in Table 1 , the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating samples with electrodeposition time of 10 and 30 min are abbreviated as SN10 and SN30, and the PTES modified coating samples with electrodeposition time of 10 min are abbreviated as SNP10. As a result, the R c of the PTES modified coating for 10 min is the largest compared with the other two groups of samples, indicating that its electrochemical performance is the best.

About the dynamic polarization curve tests, scan rate was set at 5 mV/s. The initial potential was −2.5 V and the termination potential was 0.1 V. The test results of the potentiodynamic polarization curves of four sets of magnesium samples are shown in Fig. 12 below. Electrochemical analytical software, CorrView, was employed to assess the anticorrosive capabilities of the coated surfaces in detail. The Tafel extrapolation methodology is utilized to derive the corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (i corr), the anodic and cathodic polarization Tafel slopes (β a and β c), and the corrosion rate (CR). Table 2 presents the outcomes of the Tafel curve fitting for the dynamic polarization curves for the four sets of samples.

As shown in Table 2, the corrosion rate of the PTES modified superhydrophobic electrodeposited 10 min coating sample is 2 orders of magnitude smaller than that of bare magnesium alloy, exhibiting its superior electrochemical performance. Table 2 lists the electrodeposited 10- and 30-min nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating samples, PTES modified superhydrophobic coating (electrodeposited 10 min) and Tafel fitting results of the potentiodynamic polarization curve of bare magnesium alloys. The corrosion current density (i corr) of the PTES modified samples with electrodeposition time of 10 min is the smallest, which is 2 orders of magnitude lower than that of the bare magnesium alloy, reaching 5.3936 × 10−6 A cm−2.

Adhesion test evaluates the adhesion of the coating to the metal substrate by the incision morphology of the coating after sticking and removing the pressure-sensitive adhesive tape at the incision on the surface of the coating. In this study, the "X" scratch tape test method and the "100-grid method" tape test method were used to test the adhesion ability. According to the tape test standard of "ASTMD3359-09," as shown in Fig. 13 (a), no coating is detached from the substrate in the X-shaped cutting area, and the adhesion rating of the coating is 5A. As shown in Fig. 13(b), no coating has fallen off from the substrate after inspection, the edge of the incision is completely smooth, and none of the squares of the grid are separated. The adhesion of the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating to the magnesium alloy substrate can reach 5B level.

Over an extended period, artificial superhydrophobic surfaces have exhibited low abrasion resistance [39,40], which significantly constrains the implementation of these surfaces in practical scenarios. Thus, The abrasion resistance of the PTES modified nano-SiO2/N-CDs coatings electrodeposited for 10 min were tested, and the results were shown in Fig. 13(c, d) and Fig. 14 .

As shown in Fig. 14, during the process of the friction distance gradually increasing from 0.1 to 1.3 m, the WCA of the coating did not decrease significantly. When the friction distance reached 1.5 m, the WCA of the coating slightly decreased. When the friction distance continued to gradually increase from 1.7 to 2.5 m, the WCA remained basically unchanged. When the friction distance reached 2.6 m, the WCA of the coating decreased significantly, which indicates that the hydrophobic performance decreased significantly. Therefore, the abrasion resistance of the PTES-modified 10-min superhydrophobic nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating is close to 2.6 m on sandpaper with a roughness of 2000 particle size under a pressure of 2.45 kPa.

In the previous section, we characterized the prepared coating. By analyzing the surface morphology of N-CDs powders, XRD and XPS results, it was proved that N-CDs were successfully synthesized and nano-SiO2/N-CDs were prepared on the surface of magnesium alloy by electrodeposition.
The WCA and RA of the PTES-modified coating were measured, which proved that the prepared coating exhibited good superhydrophobicity and self-cleaning performance.

The electrochemical properties of the prepared coatings were tested by EIS, potentiodynamic polarization curves and equivalent circuits, and the results showed that the conductivity of the coatings was significantly lower than that of the bare magnesium alloy matrix. The equivalent circuit showed that the PTES-modified superhydrophobic coatings had the largest resistance (Rc), and the corrosion current density increased by two orders of magnitude relative to the bare magnesium alloy matrix, indicating that the PTES-modified coatings had excellent electrochemical testing performance.

To improve the corrosion resistance of magnesium alloy, a superhydrophobic SiO2/N-CDs coating was developed onto the surface of AZ31B magnesium alloy through electrodeposition, which demonstrated good self-cleaning performance and abrasion resistance. First, N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with citric acid and benzotriazole. Then through the three-electrode system, under the action of sodium dodecyl sulfonate, the electrostatic deposition of the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating was carried out. The average thickness of the nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating for 10 and 30 min was about 218 and 469 μm, respectively. The coating exhibited excellent adhesion. The WCA of the coating for 10-min electrodeposition was 154°, and the rolling angle (RA) was 7°. The coating demonstrated good self-cleaning performance on both the plane and the inclined surface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and potentiodynamic polarization curve techniques were employed to evaluate the electrochemical performance of diverse coating systems in a scientific manner, followed by employing the equivalent circuit model for fitting purposes. The corrosion current density of the coating deposited for 10 min was 5.3936 × 10−6 A cm−2, which were both reduced by 2 orders of magnitude compared with bare AZ31B magnesium alloy, while the corrosion rate was 0.12 mm∙a−1. Finally, the abrasion resistance of PTES superhydrophobic modified nano-SiO2/N-CDs coating, which was electrodeposited by 10min, reached nearly 2.6 m on grade 2000 SiC sandpaper under a pressure of 2.45 kPa through the test. This study provides a superhydrophobic coating with good self-cleaning and abrasion resistance on AZ31B magnesium alloy matrix, which offers a method to the application of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on magnesium alloys and the self-cleaning and wear resistance of superhydrophobic coatings.

Xutong Duan: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yongfei Wang: Software. Ruijie Guo: Software. Baolong Niu: Supervision, Project administration. Huifang Wang: Software. Hong Yan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Hong Yan reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22178242) and the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (202102040201009).
